factions.name: Progressive Liberal
factions.description: Post-2015 Liberal mainstream: intersectional progressivism, active government, multilateral foreign policy, identity-conscious politics. The Trudeau synthesis of social liberalism and fiscal pragmatism.
factions.historicalContext: Dominant Liberal faction 2015–present. Defined by social progressivism, reconciliation, and internationalism.

keyPositions.Healthcare: Expand public system; pharmacare; dental care
keyPositions.Climate: Carbon pricing; clean tech investment; Paris accord
keyPositions.Foreign Policy: NATO + multilateralism; Ukraine support; rules-based order

factions.name: Bay Street Liberal
factions.description: Business-wing Liberals: pro-market, fiscally cautious, socially moderate. Descended from the Chrétien–Martin era of deficit-slaying and pro-trade policy.
factions.historicalContext: Dominated 1993–2006. Pro-NAFTA, balanced budgets, Bay Street ties.

keyPositions.Economy: Balanced budgets; public-private partnerships; corporate competitiveness
keyPositions.Trade: Free trade; USMCA; market access over protectionism
keyPositions.Healthcare: Public system with private efficiency tools

factions.name: Civil Libertarian Liberal
factions.description: Charter-focused Liberals who prioritize individual rights, privacy, and limits on state power. Skeptical of both social conservative moralizing and progressive overreach.
factions.historicalContext: Charter generation Liberals. Privacy hawks, critics of C-51, defenders of judicial independence.

keyPositions.Civil Liberties: Charter rights are near-absolute; no security-state overreach
keyPositions.Privacy: Strong data protection; oppose bulk surveillance
keyPositions.International: International law; ICC; human rights diplomacy

factions.name: Technocratic Centrist
factions.description: The emerging Carney-era Liberal: market-minded climate pragmatist, institutionalist, fiscal hawk with green investment priorities. Believes in evidence-based governance and international institutions.
factions.historicalContext: Post-Trudeau Liberal realignment toward competence politics and green growth.

keyPositions.Economy: Industrial strategy; green investment; trade diversification
keyPositions.Climate: Carbon pricing reform; clean tech; net-zero by 2050

factions.name: Social Democrat
factions.description: The NDP mainstream: Layton–Singh tradition of pragmatic social democracy. Accepts the market economy but demands a strong welfare state, workers' rights, and universal programs.
factions.historicalContext: Core NDP tradition since Tommy Douglas. Electoral peak under Layton 2011 (103 seats).

keyPositions.Healthcare: Fully public; pharmacare; dental; mental health
keyPositions.Labour: Strengthen unions; sectoral bargaining; anti-scab legislation
keyPositions.Housing: Public and non-profit housing; rent control
keyPositions.Climate: Green investment; just transition for workers

factions.name: Socialist Left
factions.description: The NDP's anti-capitalist wing: wealth redistribution, public ownership, structural economic transformation. Aligned with Bernie Sanders / AOC-style democratic socialism.
factions.historicalContext: Waffle movement in 1970s; re-emerged with Occupy and Bernie Sanders influence.

keyPositions.Economy: Wealth tax; break up corporate monopolies; public banking
keyPositions.Housing: Public housing as primary tenure form
keyPositions.Climate: Green New Deal; end fossil fuel subsidies immediately
keyPositions.Trade: Fair trade not free trade; no ISDS clauses

factions.name: Progressive Identity
factions.description: The intersectional, social justice wing: prioritizes anti-racism, Indigenous solidarity, LGBTQ+ advocacy, and decolonization over traditional class politics.
factions.historicalContext: Grew significantly post-2015 with BLM, #MeToo, and Indigenous rights movements.

keyPositions.Police: Defund police; invest in community safety alternatives
keyPositions.LGBTQ+: Trans rights; ban conversion therapy; fully funded gender-affirming care
keyPositions.Immigration: Near-open borders; no deportations; sanctuary cities
keyPositions.Racism: Systemic racism as Canada's defining structural crisis

factions.name: Economic Nationalist NDP
factions.description: The labour-nationalist wing: economic left but skeptical of globalization and corporate-led free trade. Closer to old CCF roots — workers' party first. Strong in resource communities and industrial Canada.
factions.historicalContext: Old CCF working-class base. Opposes CUSMA investor provisions; wants Canadian industrial strategy.

keyPositions.Trade: Renegotiate CUSMA; oppose ISDS; buy Canadian
keyPositions.Industry: Reindustrialize Canada; public ownership of strategic sectors
keyPositions.Immigration: Enforce labour standards; stop using TFWs to undercut wages
keyPositions.Resources: Nationalize oil sands; guarantee transition incomes
keyPositions.Sovereignty: Reduce US economic dependence; Canadian content requirements

factions.name: Western Populist
factions.description: The Poilievre coalition: anti-establishment, anti-carbon tax, pro-pipeline, populist right. Fuses economic libertarianism with cultural conservatism and strong Canadian nationalism. Hostile to 'gatekeepers' in media, housing, banking, and government.
factions.historicalContext: Reform Party legacy + Trump-era populism. Dominant in CPC since 2022. Strongest in Alberta and rural Ontario.

keyPositions.Economy: Axe the carbon tax; cut red tape; end deficit; crypto-friendly
keyPositions.Housing: Deregulate zoning; get government out of housing
keyPositions.Energy: Approve pipelines; expand oil sands; energy sovereignty
keyPositions.Crime: Mandatory minimums; more police; reverse bail reform
keyPositions.Sovereignty: Canada First on trade; push back on US pressure and Chinese interference

factions.name: Social Conservative
factions.description: The values-driven right: traditional family, faith, opposition to abortion access and gender ideology in schools. Often rural, religious, and culturally defensive. The CPC's largest faction by membership.
factions.historicalContext: Social conservatism drives CPC membership votes but is kept backstage electorally. International comparisons: Orbán-style values politics.

keyPositions.Abortion: Restrict late-term; parental consent for minors
keyPositions.LGBTQ+: Parental notification in schools; limit gender curricula
keyPositions.Drugs: Oppose decriminalization and safe supply programs
keyPositions.Family: Tax policy supporting traditional family structures

factions.name: Red Tory / Progressive Conservative
factions.description: The old Progressive Conservative tradition: fiscal conservatism with pragmatic social moderation. Accepts public healthcare, values environmental stewardship, avoids culture wars.
factions.historicalContext: The old PC Party 1942–2003. Lost the internal war to Reform/Alliance. Still crucial in Ontario swing ridings.

keyPositions.Healthcare: Efficiency reforms; some private delivery; no parallel private insurance
keyPositions.Environment: Technology solutions; lighter carbon pricing; not denial
keyPositions.Social: Pragmatic; don't legislate bedrooms; focus on economic management
keyPositions.Immigration: Moderate; economic immigration focus; integration first

factions.name: Libertarian Right
factions.description: Market fundamentalists within the CPC: minimal government, low taxes, deregulation, individual freedom over collective rights. Anti-mandate, anti-surveillance, skeptical of state power in all directions.
factions.historicalContext: Maxime Bernier wing left to form PPC in 2018. Remains influential on CPC deregulation, CBC abolition, and Bank of Canada policy.

keyPositions.Economy: Flat tax; abolish corporate welfare; end supply management entirely
keyPositions.Government: Privatize CBC; sell Canada Post; dramatically shrink federal state
keyPositions.Trade: Full free trade including dairy and cultural exemptions

factions.name: Neoconservative / Atlanticist Hawk
factions.description: The Scoop Jackson tradition applied to Canada: pro-NATO, hawkish on China and Russia, democracy promotion abroad, strong national security state. Harper's foreign policy doctrine. Fiscally right, socially moderate, defining axis is assertive foreign policy.
factions.historicalContext: Harper foreign policy 2006–2015: pro-Israel, hawkish on Iran, NATO 2%, anti-Russia. Thatcher and Reagan are international touchstones.

keyPositions.Foreign Policy: NATO 2% now; strong Israel support; confront China and Russia
keyPositions.Security: CSIS powers; foreign interference crackdown; Five Eyes integration
keyPositions.Defence: Arctic sovereignty; military modernization; NORAD upgrade
keyPositions.Trade: Ally-shoring; Indo-Pacific strategy; reduce China dependence
keyPositions.Democracy: Promote democracy abroad; sanction authoritarian states

contradictions.name: Housing Affordability vs. Anti-Development
contradictions.description: You support affordable housing but oppose market-led housing supply increases.
contradictions.explanation: Supporting affordable housing while opposing density and development typically leads to higher costs — you may want housing to be cheaper but through public provision rather than market mechanisms.
contradictions.condition: answers[3] !== undefined && answers[3] <= 1 && answers[30] !== undefined && answers[30] <= 1

resolutions.name: Public Housing Route
resolutions.text: Commit to public and non-profit housing construction as the primary supply mechanism.

resolutions.name: Market-Left Hybrid
resolutions.text: Accept some private development alongside strong rent controls and social housing.

contradictions.name: Climate Action vs. Oil Expansion
contradictions.description: You support climate action but also support expanded oil sands or Trans Mountain.
contradictions.explanation: Favouring both aggressive emissions reduction and expanded fossil fuel infrastructure creates a fundamental tension — the IEA says no new oil fields are consistent with 1.5°C.
contradictions.condition: answers[7] !== undefined && answers[7] <= 1 && answers[56] !== undefined && answers[56] >= 2

resolutions.name: Just Transition
resolutions.text: Accept that managed decline of oil sands is necessary for climate targets.

resolutions.name: Technology Optimist
resolutions.text: Believe carbon capture and clean tech will allow both oil extraction and emissions reduction.

contradictions.name: High Immigration + Housing Crisis
contradictions.description: You support high immigration levels but also want affordable housing.
contradictions.explanation: Record immigration (1.2M+/yr) puts significant pressure on housing demand. These goals create tension without a major supply-side response.
contradictions.condition: answers[30] !== undefined && answers[30] <= 1 && answers[3] !== undefined && answers[3] <= 1

resolutions.name: Supply-Side Investment
resolutions.text: Massive public housing construction can accommodate both goals simultaneously.

resolutions.name: Managed Intake
resolutions.text: Moderate immigration levels temporarily until housing supply catches up.

contradictions.name: Individual Liberty vs. Drug Enforcement
contradictions.description: You favour civil liberties and limited state power, but strict drug enforcement.
contradictions.explanation: The war on drugs involves extensive state coercion, surveillance, and incarceration — typically opposed by civil libertarians across the political spectrum.
contradictions.condition: answers[24] !== undefined && answers[24] <= 1 && answers[13] !== undefined && answers[13] >= 2

resolutions.name: Harm Reduction Liberty
resolutions.text: Apply libertarian principles consistently — decriminalize personal drug use.

resolutions.name: Community Standards
resolutions.text: Distinguish between personal freedoms and community protection standards.

contradictions.name: Fiscal Conservatism vs. Strong Military
contradictions.description: You want significant deficit reduction but also want to increase defence spending.
contradictions.explanation: Meeting NATO's 2% GDP target would add ~$15B annually to federal spending. This is hard to reconcile with urgent deficit reduction.
contradictions.condition: answers[4] !== undefined && answers[4] >= 3 && answers[35] !== undefined && answers[35] >= 2

resolutions.name: Reallocation
resolutions.text: Cut other spending to fund defence within a balanced budget.

resolutions.name: Phased Approach
resolutions.text: Accept that defence investment requires a longer fiscal timeline.

contradictions.name: Free Trade vs. Supply Management
contradictions.description: You support open free trade but also want to keep supply management.
contradictions.explanation: Supply management is a quota and tariff system that contradicts free trade principles. Every trade partner demands its removal. You can't fully hold both positions.
contradictions.condition: answers[31] !== undefined && answers[31] <= 1 && answers[44] !== undefined && answers[44] <= 1

resolutions.name: Managed Trade
resolutions.text: Accept that strategic sectors can be exempt from free trade rules.

resolutions.name: Compensated Reform
resolutions.text: Phase out supply management with transition support for farmers.

contradictions.name: Skeptical of State Power vs. Carbon Tax Support
contradictions.description: You distrust government authority but support a carbon pricing mechanism.
contradictions.explanation: Carbon pricing is a form of state-imposed economic intervention. Libertarian-minded conservatives sometimes hold this position but it creates philosophical tension.
contradictions.condition: answers[24] !== undefined && answers[24] >= 2 && answers[7] !== undefined && answers[7] <= 1

resolutions.name: Market Mechanism
resolutions.text: A revenue-neutral carbon tax is actually more market-friendly than direct regulation.

resolutions.name: Voluntary Alternative
resolutions.text: Prefer voluntary carbon offsets and technology investment over mandatory pricing.

contradictions.name: Strong Nationalism vs. Free Trade
contradictions.description: You prioritize Canadian sovereignty and nationalism but also support open free trade.
contradictions.explanation: Free trade agreements require ceding sovereignty over economic policy. Trump's tariffs show that trade dependence is a sovereignty risk — nationalists typically prefer protectionism.
contradictions.condition: answers[31] !== undefined && answers[31] <= 1 && answers[32] !== undefined && answers[32] >= 2

resolutions.name: Economic Nationalist
resolutions.text: Consistent nationalism requires protecting domestic industries through managed trade.

resolutions.name: Sovereign Market
resolutions.text: Free trade is compatible with sovereignty when you have economic leverage.
